title: Sourcegraph user gateway tokens should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Sourcegraph user gateway token detected in source code. Sourcegraph user gateway tokens (sgu_ prefix) provide authenticated access to Sourcegraph gateway services and user-specific API endpoints, enabling integration with Sourcegraph's code intelligence platform through gateway proxies and user-level service access. These tokens grant various permissions including: authenticating user requests through Sourcegraph gateway services, accessing user-specific API endpoints and services, managing user-level integrations and connections, accessing code search through gateway proxies, managing user preferences and settings via gateway, accessing user-level code intelligence features, managing user authentication and session tokens, accessing user-specific repository connections, managing user-level webhooks and integrations, accessing user account information and profile data, managing user-level API quotas and rate limits, accessing user notification and alert services, managing user-level code monitors and alerts, accessing user collaboration and sharing features, managing user-level search contexts and filters, accessing user dashboard and analytics data, managing user browser extension and IDE integration settings, accessing user-level batch changes and campaigns, managing user code review and approval workflows, and accessing user-level audit logs and activity history. Exposing Sourcegraph user gateway tokens enables attackers to: impersonate the user in all gateway-authenticated requests, access user's private repositories and code through gateway services, search and browse all code accessible to the compromised user account, access sensitive information and credentials embedded in user's accessible code, modify user settings and preferences through gateway APIs, create unauthorized integrations in the user's name, access user's code intelligence data and search history, manipulate user-level code monitors and alerts, access user collaboration history and shared content, exfiltrate user's saved searches and search contexts, modify user browser extension and IDE integration settings, access user dashboard revealing usage patterns and accessed repositories, create malicious batch changes affecting user's repositories, manipulate code review workflows and approvals, access user notification settings and sensitive alerts, gather intelligence about user's development activities and projects, pivot to connected services through user integrations, establish persistence through user-level webhooks, consume user API quotas causing service denial, and escalate privileges by combining with other exposed credentials. This represents a significant user account security risk requiring immediate action. Take immediate action: revoke the exposed user gateway token immediately from user settings, generate a new user gateway token with minimal required permissions, update all user integrations and automation with the new token, conduct comprehensive audit of user account access logs for unauthorized gateway activity, review all user-level settings and preferences for modifications, verify no unauthorized integrations or webhooks were created, check user's code monitors and alerts for tampering, review user's saved searches and search contexts for unauthorized access, audit user's recent search history and accessed repositories, verify browser extension and IDE integration settings remain secure, review user collaboration activities for unauthorized sharing, check user-level batch changes for malicious modifications, audit code review activities and approval workflows, verify user notification settings and alert configurations, implement principle of least privilege for user gateway tokens, store user gateway tokens in secure password managers or credential vaults, use environment variables for user automation and integration tokens, never commit user gateway tokens to any repository including personal projects, implement comprehensive secret scanning across all user repositories and scripts, rotate user gateway tokens on regular schedule (every 90 days recommended), enable multi-factor authentication for token generation and management, implement IP restrictions for user gateway token usage where possible, monitor and alert on user gateway token usage from unexpected locations, maintain inventory of all user gateway tokens with their purpose and usage, implement approval workflows for creating new user gateway tokens, regularly audit user gateway token permissions and access patterns, educate users about gateway token security and proper handling procedures, integrate user gateway token detection in personal development workflows, implement automated token rotation for user-level integrations where supported, and conduct regular security reviews of user gateway token usage and access controls.
